title: Lamb Chop Fond Potatoes (Cast Iron)
subtitle: Par-cooked Yukon Golds crisped in leftover lamb fat and fond, then finished with garlic, parsley, and a little lemon.

ingredients.heading: Ingredients
ingredients.note: Best made right after lamb chops, using the same skillet. Keep the good brown fond; ditch any bitter black bits.
items: 1 1/2-2 lb Yukon Gold potatoes, halved if small or cut into 1 1/2-inch chunks
Kosher salt
1/2 tsp baking soda (optional, but helps create rough crisp edges)
2-3 tbsp lamb fat + fond left in the skillet after cooking lamb chops
1 tbsp butter (optional, for the finish)
1 small garlic clove, very finely minced or grated
2-3 tbsp flat-leaf parsley, finely chopped
1-2 tsp fresh lemon juice
Black pepper (optional)
ingredients.callout: Fallback: If you don't have enough drippings in the pan, add a spoonful of rendered lamb fat or olive oil so the potatoes fry instead of sticking.

body.html: This is the best use of the skillet after lamb chops come out to rest: keep a thin layer of fat, preserve the tasty brown fond, and crisp par-cooked potatoes until the edges turn deep golden and shaggy.

items: Par-cook potatoes in well-salted water
Drain + rough up the surfaces for extra crisp edges
Leave 2-3 tbsp fat + fond in the lamb-chop skillet
Sear, then oven-finish the potatoes in a single layer
Finish with garlic, parsley, and lemon right at the end

body.html: Important: Brown fond is flavor. Burned black bits are bitterness. If the pan smells acrid, wipe out the worst of it and keep only the clean browned drippings.

slides.title: Par-Cook the Potatoes
slides.checkboxLabel: Boil until just tender, then drain well

items: Bring a pot of water to a boil and season it until it tastes lightly salty.
Add potatoes and the baking soda if using.
Simmer 10-14 minutes, until a knife slides in easily but the pieces still hold together.
Drain well and let the potatoes steam off for 2-3 minutes.

body.html: Why par-cook first: you get fluffy centers without having to steam the skillet later, which keeps the fond from washing away.

slides.title: Rough Up + Preheat
slides.checkboxLabel: Toss to rough the edges; heat oven to 425F

items: Shake the drained potatoes in the colander or toss them in a bowl until the outsides look a little scruffy.
Season with 1/2 tsp kosher salt to start.
Preheat the oven to 425F while the potatoes dry a bit more.

body.html: Texture trick: rough, starchy edges crisp better than smooth ones.

slides.title: Set Up the Lamb-Chop Skillet
slides.checkboxLabel: Leave 2-3 tbsp drippings and scrape up the good fond

items: Cook your lamb chops first, then transfer them to a plate to rest.
Pour off excess fat, but leave 2-3 tbsp drippings plus the browned bits in the skillet.
Set the pan over medium-high for a minute or two.
If the fond looks stuck and dry, add 1 tbsp water around the edge and scrape just enough to loosen the browned bits into the fat.

body.html: You want a thin glossy film in the skillet, not a pool. Too much fat makes the potatoes greasy instead of crisp.

slides.title: Crisp in the Skillet, Then the Oven
slides.checkboxLabel: Sear cut-side down, then roast until deeply golden

items: Add potatoes in a single layer and press the cut sides into the pan.
Cook 4-6 minutes without fussing until the first side picks up real color.
Turn the potatoes once, then transfer the skillet to the oven.
Roast 12-18 minutes, tossing once or twice, until the edges are deeply browned and crisp.

body.html: Don't crowd the pan. If the skillet is packed tight, work in batches. Space is what gives you crust.

slides.title: Finish With Garlic + Herbs
slides.checkboxLabel: Bloom garlic briefly, then toss with parsley and lemon

items: Return the skillet to the stove over medium-low if needed.
Push potatoes to the edges, add butter in the center if using, then add the garlic and stir 10-15 seconds until fragrant.
Toss the garlic through the potatoes, turn off the heat, then add parsley + lemon juice.
Taste and adjust with more salt and a little black pepper if you want.

body.html: Finish off heat: garlic should smell sweet and fragrant, not brown; lemon wakes up the lamb richness without making the potatoes taste sharp.
